_meta.draft_status: initial draft by Claude Code, Thu Apr 23 2026 — requires curator review for pedagogical accuracy
_meta.research_basis: Synthesized from Van de Walle 'Elementary and Middle School Mathematics', Common Core Progressions documents for fractions (3-5), and published research on rational number misconceptions (Behr, Lesh, Post; Mack; Siegler). Nothing copied verbatim.
notes_for_barbara: Each misconception has: id, name, short_description, manifestation (how it shows up in student work), grade_onset (when it typically appears), diagnostic_signals (what specific wrong answers or work patterns reveal it), prerequisite_links (which prerequisite gaps can masquerade as this misconception).
prerequisite_links are what the Plan Architect uses for differential diagnosis (spec §4.4).
8 misconceptions drafted — spec said 6-8. Drop m08 if you want only 7; it partially overlaps with m04.

misconceptions.name: Bigger denominator means bigger fraction
misconceptions.short_description: Learner treats fractions as whole numbers, so 1/8 seems bigger than 1/4 because 8 > 4.
misconceptions.manifestation: When comparing unit fractions or fractions with the same numerator, picks the one with the larger denominator. Often persists into comparing 3/4 and 3/8, picking 3/8.
misconceptions.grade_onset: Grade 3, when fractions are formally introduced. Common and sticky.
diagnostic_signals: Selects 1/8 over 1/4 when asked which is bigger.
In show-your-work: 'because 8 is bigger than 4'.
On a number line task, places 1/8 farther from 0 than 1/4.
prerequisite_links: p01_whole_number_magnitude (whole-number comparison fluency is fine; the issue is transfer, not the prerequisite)

misconceptions.name: Add numerators and denominators separately
misconceptions.short_description: Adds fractions by adding tops and bottoms independently: 1/2 + 1/3 = 2/5.
misconceptions.manifestation: Applies whole-number addition piece-wise across the fraction bar without any equivalence reasoning. Can look procedurally 'right' to the learner because each step mimics familiar whole-number addition.
misconceptions.grade_onset: Grade 4, when addition of fractions begins. Very common when unlike denominators are introduced.
diagnostic_signals: Show-your-work often shows two vertical additions: 1+1=2 and 2+3=5.
Sum is systematically smaller than either addend (impossible for positive fractions).
prerequisite_links: p02_equivalent_fractions (the fix requires understanding equivalent fractions, which may be the real gap)

misconceptions.name: Fractions are always less than 1
misconceptions.short_description: Learner believes a fraction must be between 0 and 1, so doesn't recognize improper fractions or fractions greater than a whole.
misconceptions.manifestation: Refuses to write 5/4 or treats it as an error. When asked 'is 7/3 a fraction?', says no or converts immediately to 2 1/3 without acknowledging 7/3 as a valid form. May believe the numerator must be less than the denominator.
misconceptions.grade_onset: Grade 3-4, especially when introducing improper fractions or fractions on a number line beyond 1.
diagnostic_signals: Writes 2 R1 instead of 7/3 when dividing.
Places all fractions between 0 and 1 on a number line, even when asked to place 5/2.
Says 'that's not a fraction' about 6/4.
prerequisite_links: p03_part_whole_beyond_one (understanding that you can have more than a whole, divided into parts)

misconceptions.name: Doesn't recognize equivalent fractions visually or conceptually
misconceptions.short_description: Treats 2/4 and 1/2 as unrelated because they look different. Doesn't yet see that different fractions can name the same quantity.
misconceptions.manifestation: When shown a rectangle split into 4 parts with 2 shaded, labels it 2/4 but not 1/2. When asked if 2/4 equals 1/2, says no or is unsure. Shades 2/4 and 1/2 of identical shapes and says the shaded regions are different sizes.
misconceptions.grade_onset: Grade 3, when equivalence is first introduced. Can persist into grade 4 without targeted work.
diagnostic_signals: Says 2/4 ≠ 1/2 when asked directly.
Draws 2/4 and 1/2 and says they are not the same amount.
Can compute equivalent-fraction tables mechanically but can't explain why they are equal.
prerequisite_links: p04_multiplicative_reasoning (equivalence requires multiplicative thinking — multiplying top and bottom by the same number)

misconceptions.name: Partitioning doesn't require equal parts
misconceptions.short_description: Divides a shape into any number of parts — unequal in size — and treats them as fractional parts.
misconceptions.manifestation: Asked to shade 1/4 of a rectangle, divides the rectangle into 4 unequal parts and shades one. Asked to show 1/3, partitions with eyeballed cuts that are visibly uneven.
misconceptions.grade_onset: Grade 2-3 pre-fractions and early grade 3. Often the underlying issue when a kid 'knows what a fraction is' but answers visual problems wrong.
diagnostic_signals: Partitions a circle into 4 wedges of clearly different sizes and says each is 1/4.
Shades one of those unequal parts when asked for 1/4.
Doesn't self-correct when the uneven partition is pointed out visually.
prerequisite_links: p05_equal_partitioning (this is both a fractions misconception and a prerequisite-gap candidate — the Plan Architect should flag it as a possible prerequisite issue)

misconceptions.name: The whole doesn't matter
misconceptions.short_description: Thinks 1/2 is always the same amount regardless of what whole it refers to. Ignores the referent.
misconceptions.manifestation: Says '1/2 of a small pizza is the same as 1/2 of a large pizza' in a context where size comparisons matter. Says 1/4 > 1/2 if shown a larger shape split into fourths and a smaller shape split into halves.
misconceptions.grade_onset: Grade 3-4, surfaces in applied/word problems where the whole varies.
diagnostic_signals: In a word problem comparing fractions of different wholes, compares the fractions as if the wholes were equal.
Can't explain why 1/2 of something depends on what that something is.
Correctly compares fractions of the same whole but fails when wholes differ.
prerequisite_links: p06_referent_unit (understanding that every fraction refers to a specific whole, and comparison requires the same whole)

misconceptions.name: Notational confusion — reads 3/4 as 'three, four'
misconceptions.short_description: Doesn't yet read fraction notation as a single quantity. Reads 3/4 as two numbers or says 'three over four' without understanding it as one value.
misconceptions.manifestation: Asked to read 3/4 aloud, says 'three four' or 'three slash four'. In show-your-work, treats 3 and 4 as independent numbers. May struggle to place 3/4 on a number line because it's 'two numbers'.
misconceptions.grade_onset: Grade 3 early fractions. Usually resolves quickly with explicit instruction, but persists when fraction work is procedural and notation isn't explicitly grounded.
diagnostic_signals: Reads 3/4 as 'three, four' or 'three four'.
In work, writes 3/4 + 1/4 as '3 plus 1 and 4 plus 4'.
Can't identify the numerator or denominator role.
prerequisite_links: p07_fraction_as_single_quantity (understanding a fraction names one number, not two)

misconceptions.name: Makes equivalent fractions by adding instead of multiplying
misconceptions.short_description: To find an equivalent fraction, adds the same number to numerator and denominator: 2/3 = 4/5 because +2 to each.
misconceptions.manifestation: When asked for a fraction equivalent to 2/3, writes 3/4 or 4/5 (adding 1 or 2 to each). May get 4/6 right sometimes because +2 coincidentally produces a valid equivalent with small numbers. Does not understand the multiplicative structure.
misconceptions.grade_onset: Grade 4, when equivalence procedures are being taught and learners substitute additive reasoning because multiplicative reasoning is not yet fluent.
diagnostic_signals: Show-your-work shows '+1 top, +1 bottom' pattern.
Can't verify equivalence with an area model — their 'equivalent' fractions produce visibly different shaded regions.
prerequisite_links: p04_multiplicative_reasoning (core gap: multiplicative vs additive thinking about fractions)

prerequisites.name: Whole-number magnitude comparison
prerequisites.description: Understands which of two whole numbers is larger. Typically mastered by grade 2. Rarely the real gap in fractions work, but worth a quick probe if m01 is flagged persistently.
prerequisites.probe_strategy: 3-5 whole-number comparisons mixed with fractional ones.

prerequisites.name: Fluency with equivalent fractions (as distinct from the misconception)
prerequisites.description: Can generate and recognize equivalent fractions using multiplicative reasoning. This is simultaneously a sub-skill of fractions AND a prerequisite for fluent addition/subtraction with unlike denominators.
prerequisites.probe_strategy: Equivalence generation tasks and visual equivalence recognition.

prerequisites.name: Part-whole relationships beyond a single whole
prerequisites.description: Understands that quantities can exceed one whole and be represented as parts (e.g., one and a half pizzas, or 3/2).
prerequisites.probe_strategy: Applied problems involving more than one whole divided into parts.

prerequisites.name: Multiplicative reasoning with whole numbers
prerequisites.description: Times-tables fluency and understanding of multiplication as scaling (not just repeated addition). Critical for equivalence and for later proportional reasoning.
prerequisites.probe_strategy: Multiplication fluency probe plus 1-2 scaling word problems.

prerequisites.name: Equal partitioning of a whole
prerequisites.description: Can divide a shape or length into a specified number of equal parts, both physically and by reasoning. Pre-fractions skill.
prerequisites.probe_strategy: Partitioning tasks with shapes and number lines.

prerequisites.name: Understanding the referent whole
prerequisites.description: Grasps that a fraction is always a fraction of something, and the 'something' matters. Underlies valid comparison and addition.
prerequisites.probe_strategy: Compare-across-wholes problems with explicit whole size differences.

prerequisites.name: Fraction as a single quantity
prerequisites.description: Reads a fraction as one number, not two. Can locate it on a number line.
prerequisites.probe_strategy: Number-line placement tasks and read-aloud prompts.
